Germination test method: count out a fixed sample (e.g. 10, 20, or 100 seeds), sprout them between damp cloth/paper kept warm, then compute germination percentage = sprouted / total x 100.

Uses the germination result to act: high % keep and sow normally; low % sow more thickly to compensate or discard and replace the lot - and notes typical viability ranges by crop (onion/parsnip seed is short-lived ~1-2 yr; beans/grains/tomato keep many years).
Governance for withdrawal: a logbook/registry recording who took what variety and how much, so stock is tracked and accountable.

Replenishment rule: borrowers return more seed than they took after harvest (e.g. return a multiple of the amount withdrawn) so the bank stays stocked and viability is refreshed with new-season seed; old stock is rotated out.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends storing seed while damp/not fully dried, or in a warm/humid location, or sealing un-dried seed airtight (causes mould and rapid loss of viability).
Claims seed viability does not decline with storage time, or omits any way to check viability before relying on the seed.
reference_facts: Seed storage principle: dry, cool, dark. Moisture and heat are the primary killers of seed viability.
Harrington's rule: seed longevity roughly halves for each ~5 C increase in storage temperature and each ~1% increase in seed moisture content (valid roughly 0-50 C, ~5-14% moisture).
Short-term rule of thumb: storage RH (%) + temperature (F) should sum below ~100.
Long-term seed storage needs airtight containers and low moisture; desiccants (dry wood ash, charcoal, rice, silica) help keep seed dry.
Germination % = (number sprouted / number tested) x 100, from a counted sample sprouted on damp paper/cloth.
Seed longevity varies: onion/parsnip seed is short-lived (~1-2 years); beans, grains, tomato, and many brassicas remain viable for several years when stored dry and cool.

title: Seed-save maize vs beans without inbreeding or crossing
scenario: Long-term settlement maintaining two staple seed lines: an open-pollinated flint maize and a heirloom dry bean (Phaseolus vulgaris).
A neighbouring camp ~250 m away grows a different maize variety; both groups must keep their lines pure across years.
Limited land; the saver wants the smallest plant numbers and spacing that still keep each line genetically healthy and true-to-type.
prompt: I'm the seed-keeper for our settlement and I have to maintain two staple lines for the long term without them degenerating: an open-pollinated flint MAIZE and an heirloom dry BEAN (Phaseolus vulgaris). I need exact, defensible numbers. For each crop tell me: (1) the minimum number of plants I must save seed from each year to avoid inbreeding depression, and WHY the two crops need such different numbers; (2) the isolation distance I need to keep my line pure from a different variety, given that a neighbour camp grows a DIFFERENT maize about 250 m away; (3) which crop is the urgent cross-contamination problem and which is nearly self-isolating, and why. I also want to know how I'd recognise inbreeding depression in the maize over a few seasons, and at least one practical trick (other than raw distance) to stop the neighbour's maize pollen from contaminating mine. Give the mechanism behind every number.
rubric: States maize is an obligate wind-pollinated outcrosser (monoecious, protandrous) and therefore suffers severe inbreeding depression, while common bean is a self-pollinating (cleistogamous, perfect-flowered) crop that tolerates tiny populations.

Gives a minimum maize saving population on the order of ~100-200 plants (≥100, ideally ~200) and explains it is to maintain effective population size / allelic diversity and avoid inbreeding depression.

Gives a much smaller bean saving population, on the order of ~10-20 plants (some say as few as 5-10 for maintenance, ~20 for genetic preservation), because selfers are already highly homozygous and do not suffer meaningful inbreeding depression.

States that maize requires a large isolation distance from other maize varieties, roughly 200 m as a practical minimum and up to ~400 m / 800 ft (or further, up to ~0.5 mile/1.6 km for purity), because pollen is wind-borne over long distances.
Identifies that the neighbour's maize at ~250 m is a real cross-contamination risk to the maize line (distance is marginal/insufficient for high purity), so additional control is needed.

States the bean needs only a very small isolation distance (on the order of 3-6 m / 10-20 ft) from other bean varieties because it self-pollinates before/with flower opening; the bean line is essentially self-isolating.
Identifies maize as the urgent crossing problem and beans as nearly self-isolating, attributing the difference to wind pollination vs. self-pollination/cleistogamy.

Describes a non-distance control for maize, e.g. time-isolation (staggered planting so tassels/silks of the two varieties shed at different times) and/or hand-pollination with bagged tassels and silks.

Describes recognising maize inbreeding depression across seasons as declining vigour: reduced height, weaker stands, poor germination, smaller ears, barren/partly filled cobs, and lower yield over generations.
Notes practical hygiene that strengthens the line: save seed from many plants (not just the few best ears) to keep diversity, and rogue out off-type or diseased plants before pollen shed.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends saving maize seed from only a handful of plants (e.g. a few best cobs) as adequate to keep the line healthy long-term.
Claims maize and beans need the same isolation distance, or that beans need a large isolation distance from other bean varieties.
reference_facts: Maize (Zea mays) is monoecious, protandrous, and obligately wind-cross-pollinated; it shows strong inbreeding depression, so seed savers are advised to keep ~100-200 plants minimum (commonly cited 'save from at least 100-200 plants').

Common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris) has perfect, largely cleistogamous self-pollinating flowers; it is highly homozygous and tolerates very small populations (~5-20 plants), and only ~3-6 m (10-20 ft) isolation between varieties is needed.

Maize isolation: cross-pollination is substantial within 200 m and approaches zero near 300 m; recommended isolation for open-pollinated purity is ~200 m up to ~400 m (800 ft) or further (~0.5 mile) for strict purity.
Time isolation (staggered sowing so pollen shed periods don't overlap) and bagging/hand-pollination are accepted alternatives to distance isolation for maize.
Inbreeding depression in maize manifests as reduced height, vigour, germination, ear size, kernel set, and yield across generations.
Saving seed from many parents (not a few ears) preserves allelic diversity and effective population size; roguing off-types before pollen shed maintains trueness-to-type.
